mergeRationale: The guard only blocks protocol-relative (//) URLs; absolute http:// and https:// URLs still override baseURL and carry the internal Authorization header to an arbitrary host.

requiredFindings.summary: The patch blocks only protocol-relative URLs and still permits http:// or https:// absolute URLs.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: http://evil.test and https://evil.test are also absolute URLs that make Axios ignore baseURL, so a caller-controlled absolute URL sends the internal Authorization header to an attacker host (credential leak) or an internal address (SSRF).
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Parse the final URL and reject any origin outside the configured baseURL, or disable absolute URLs for this client (e.g. allowAbsoluteUrls: false).

requiredFindings.summary: Tests cover //evil.test but not https://evil.test or http://evil.test.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: The vulnerable absolute-URL path is still untested, and nothing asserts the Authorization header is not sent off-host.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Add tests for absolute scheme URLs, mixed-case schemes, encoded values, and header leakage.

disallowedConclusions: The patch is safe because protocol-relative URLs are now rejected.
startsWith('//') is enough to keep requests on baseURL.
